summary: Puerto Rico is where my father and grandmother were born and raised. My grandmother, Mami Sonja, grew up in the projects of Guaynabo, Puerto Rico, in a poverty-ridden area during the 1950s and 60s. Although she never imagined leaving the island, she knew that moving away would open up opportunities. My father spent his early childhood in Puerto Rico, specifically in Santurce, before moving to the mainland United States at age five. Both of them have a deep connection to Puerto Rico, even though they no longer feel they fully belong there.

My family moved from Puerto Rico to the United States, specifically to Charleston, South Carolina, when my father was just five years old. Even though Puerto Ricans are U.S. citizens, my grandmother and father faced discrimination and cultural barriers here, like language struggles and racism. My grandmother worked for thirty years at Child Protective Services in the States, serving the community despite often being treated with intolerance. Our family blends Puerto Rican and American cultures, celebrating holidays with food and language from both places.

Guaynabo, Puerto Rico, is where my grandmother, Mami Sonja, was born in 1954 and grew up in the projects. The area was very poor, and many people, including her mother, were illiterate. Despite these challenges, Mami Sonja worked hard to earn a full-ride scholarship to a private school in Guaynabo, where she learned English fluently by high school. Her experiences there shaped her passion for fighting injustice and advocating for Puerto Rican independence.

The Vietnam War had a profound impact on my family and Puerto Rico as a whole. My grandmother witnessed the injustice of the 'blood tax,' where Puerto Ricans were drafted into the U.S. Army despite many not speaking English. Two of her family members served in the war, and tragically, one did not return. This fueled her activism, leading her to join the FUPI and participate in protests advocating for Puerto Rican independence during that era.

The United States Army played a significant role in my family's history, especially during the Vietnam War. Two of my grandmother's family members were drafted into the Army, despite language barriers, and one of them lost his life. Ironically, my grandmother later married an American soldier and moved to Charleston with her children. The Army's presence in our story highlights the complex relationship Puerto Ricans have with the U.S.

FUPI, the Puerto Rican independence organization, was something my grandmother got involved with after witnessing injustices like the blood tax during the Vietnam War. She attended many of their protests, even when they turned dangerous, because she believed strongly in fighting for Puerto Rico's independence from the United States. Joining FUPI was part of her activism and passion for justice during her younger years in Puerto Rico.

Charleston, South Carolina, became my family's home when my grandmother moved here with her three children, including my father, after marrying an American soldier. My father grew up in Charleston, where he faced challenges adapting but eventually found a community through music. It was also where my grandmother worked for thirty years at Child Protective Services, serving the local community despite facing discrimination. Charleston is where much of our family's American experience unfolded.

My grandmother worked at Child Protective Services in Charleston for thirty years as both a case worker and an interpreter. Her passion for fighting injustice and helping those who couldn't always fight for themselves drove her commitment to this work. Even with decades of service, she often faced intolerance due to her accent and background, like being asked for her green card at the DMV. Her dedication to the community is a big part of her story here in the States.

Santurce, Puerto Rico, is where my father spent his early childhood before moving to the United States at age five. He missed his hometown and his father, who stayed behind in Santurce, and struggled with learning English after the move. Although his memories of Santurce are fading since he stopped visiting after his father's death when he was thirteen, it remains an important part of his identity and early life.

Hip hop music was one of my father's favorite genres growing up in Charleston. He appreciated hip hop for its storytelling aspect, which helped him connect with the culture and language of the United States. This genre was part of how he learned English and found a way to express himself during his challenging early years in a new country.

Punk rock was another favorite music genre of my father, who said he liked it because it was 'angry like me.' When he was fifteen, he started a punk band called 'Aggravated' in Charleston, where he wrote lyrics, sang, and played lead guitar. Punk rock gave him a way to channel his feelings and connect with a local underground scene, even leading to conflicts with rival bands. His punk rock days ended after I was born, when he shifted to a more domestic role.

'Aggravated' was the punk rock band my father started when he was fifteen in Charleston. He was the lead singer, guitarist, and lyricist, and the band became well-known in the local underground punk scene. They often clashed with a rival neo-Nazi band, which led to regular brawls due to xenophobic remarks. The band was a significant part of my father's youth before he moved on to focus on family life after I was born.
